I Built a Browser-Based YouTube Frame Extractor (No FFmpeg Required)
I used to do this every time I needed frames from a YouTube video
I used to run this workflow every time I needed frames from a YouTube video:
youtube-dl <url> -o video.mp4
ffmpeg -i video.mp4 -vf fps=2 frames/frame_%04d.jpg
Download the entire video. Run FFmpeg. Wait. Sort through hundreds of files. Delete the video afterward.
It worked — but it always felt inefficient. The video was already playing in my browser, decoded and rendered frame by frame. Why download it again just to extract what was already on the screen?
So I built something different.
The Idea
Press enter or click to view image in full size

YouTube already decodes every frame using the browser’s <video> element. Instead of downloading and decoding the video again, YTFR : YT Fr4m3 R1pp3r captures rendered frames directly from the browser.
· No downloads
· No server
· No API keys
· No uploads
How It Works
Open YouTube Video → Click START AUTO-RIP → Capture Frames → Store as Blobs → Package with JSZip → Download ZIP
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Key Features
· Automatic frame extraction
· Manual frame capture
· Contact sheet generation
· CSV & JSON reports
· Draggable UI
· Touch support
· Memory protection
Challenges
· Handling YouTube SPA navigation
· Supporting browsers without requestVideoFrameCallback()
· Managing RAM usage
· Keeping the UI persistent
GitHub
